en.extract:
The crimson fruitcrow (Haematoderus militaris) is a species of bird in the large family Cotingidae, not a crow. The only member of the genus Haematoderus, it is found in Brazil, French Guiana, Guyana, Suriname, and Venezuela.
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests. Because of its large range and population density this species is not classified as vulnerable.


Description
The crimson fruitcrow is a large bird about 34 centimetres (13 in) long. The adult male has glossy crimson plumage on head, back and breast, the feathers being long and stiff. A bushy crimson crest is sometimes raised. The wings and tail are dark brown to black, the tail being partly concealed by the long tail-coverts. The female and the immature male have a paler crimson head, throat and underparts, the back, wings and tail being dark brown and the feathers being more flexible. Both sexes have thick, dark red beaks.


Distribution
This species is native to the tropical, lowland rainforests of Guyana, French Guiana and northeastern Brazil. There have been occasional sightings outside its normal range, in west Brazil and east Venezuela. It is an uncommon species and is present in the forest canopy and at the verges of woodland.


Behaviour
Despite its name, this fruitcrow feeds mainly on large insects such as cicadas, beetles and grasshoppers; it supplements this diet with fruit. Its flight is leisurely and looping, and a display flight, involving ascending in a corkscrew fashion followed by a long glide, has been documented. It perches high in the canopy, each bird seeming to have a favoured spot. Although usually silent, this bird can emit a low hoot reminiscent of an owl, and also has a sharp "bok" call.


Status
The crimson fruitcrow is generally rather uncommon and patchy in its distribution, and its population size may be declining slowly. However, it has a very extensive range, and the International Union for Conservation of Nature does not consider it threatened and has assessed its conservation status as being of "least concern".


References

The '''crimson fruitcrow''' ('''''Haematoderus militaris''''') is a [[species]] of bird in the large family [[Cotingidae]], not a [[crow]].<ref>{{cite book|author=Sclater, Philip Lutley |title=Catalogue of a Collection of American Birds|url=https://archive.org/details/catalogueofcolle00scla |year=1862 |publisher=N. Trubner and Company|pages=[https://archive.org/details/catalogueofcolle00scla/page/238 238], 257}}</ref> The [[Monotypic taxon|only member]] of the [[genus]] '''''Haematoderus''''', it is found in [[Brazil]], [[French Guiana]], [[Guyana]], [[Suriname]], and [[Venezuela]].
Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s. Because of its large [[Range (biology)|range]] and [[population density]] this species is not classified as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=4518 |title=Birdlife International |access-date=2013-01-18 |archive-date=2014-07-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140714173339/http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=4518 }}</ref>

==Description==
The crimson fruitcrow is a large bird about {{convert|34|cm|in|0}} long. The adult male has glossy crimson plumage on head, back and breast, the feathers being long and stiff. A bushy crimson crest is sometimes raised. The wings and tail are dark brown to black, the tail being partly concealed by the long tail-coverts. The female and the immature male have a paler crimson head, throat and underparts, the back, wings and tail being dark brown and the feathers being more flexible. Both sexes have thick, dark red beaks.<ref name=Ridgely>{{cite book|author1=Ridgely, Robert S. |author2=Tudor, Guy |title=Field Guide to the Songbirds of South America: The Passerines |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gC_A2fpBl1gC&pg=PA312 |year=2009 |publisher=University of Texas Press |isbn=978-0-292-71748-0 |page=512}}</ref><ref name=Ridgely1989>{{cite book|author1=Ridgely, Robert S. |author2=Tudor, Guy |title=The Birds of South America: Vol. II, The Suboscine Passerines |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FPrCwEeHGkIC&pg=PA772 |year=1989 |publisher=University of Texas Press |isbn=978-0-292-77063-8 |pages=772–773}}</ref>

==Distribution==
This species is native to the tropical, lowland rainforests of Guyana, French Guiana and northeastern Brazil. There have been occasional sightings outside its normal range, in west Brazil and east Venezuela. It is an uncommon species and is present in the forest canopy and at the verges of woodland.<ref name=Ridgely1989/>

==Behaviour==
Despite its name, this fruitcrow feeds mainly on large insects such as [[cicada]]s, [[beetle]]s and [[grasshopper]]s; it supplements this diet with fruit.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.hbw.com/node/57047 |title=Crimson Fruitcrow (''Haematoderus militaris'') |author1=Snow, D. |author2=Sharpe, C.J. |year=2015 |work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |publisher=Lynx Edicions, Barcelona |access-date=2015-08-30}}</ref> Its flight is leisurely and looping, and a display flight, involving ascending in a corkscrew fashion followed by a long glide, has been documented. It perches high in the [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]], each bird seeming to have a favoured spot. Although usually silent, this bird can emit a low hoot reminiscent of an owl, and also has a sharp "bok" call.<ref name=Ridgely1989/>

==Status==
The crimson fruitcrow is generally rather uncommon and patchy in its distribution, and its population size may be declining slowly. However, it has a very extensive range, and the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] does not consider it threatened and has assessed its conservation status as being of "[[Least-concern species|least concern]]".<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />
